text: The patient is positioned securely on the operating table, which is tilted to the right side. The surgeon elevates the omentum over the transverse colon and packs the small bowel onto the right side of the abdomen, enhancing exposure of the splenic flexure for the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein at the inferior edge of the pancreas, lateral to the ligament of Treitz. The peritoneum is divided just below the vein, creating a dissection window underneath it. The surgeon carefully identifies the appropriate dissection plane high in the area to begin mobilization.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection at the splenic flexure, carefully teasing apart tissue planes with minimal bleeding, maintaining the correct dissection plane using the medial approach.

The surgeon continues dissection, identifying three landmarks: the inferior mesenteric artery at the inferior edge, extending the dissection laterally and cranially. The pulsations of the left renal artery are visible during this pause.
The surgeon completes the cranial mobilization upon reaching the inferior margin of the pancreas. The lateral dissection is finalized as the descending colon becomes visible.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization by incising the peritoneum along the white line of TOLT, entering the pre-existing medial dissection window. The dissection is carried up to the inferior pole of the spleen. The omentum is detached from the middle of the transverse colon, allowing entry into the lesser sac.
The surgeon identifies the posterior wall of the stomach to confirm positioning within the lesser sac. The dissection is extended laterally to connect with the previously mobilized lateral side.

The surgeon applies upward traction on the omentum to prevent tension on the spleen, minimizing the risk of capsular injuries. The dissection proceeds across the base of the transverse colon mesentery, stopping before the left branch of the middle colic vessels. The inferior mesenteric vein is isolated at the inferior border of the pancreas and divided.

The surgeon divides any mesentery or fusion between the distal transverse colon and proximal descending colon, taking care to protect the arch of Riolan and ensure perfusion of the distal colon. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized, allowing the surgeon to proceed with the distal resection.
